summary:
The fig (Ficus carica) was among the earliest fruit trees to be cultivated by human societies in the Near East, with some evidence suggesting intentional propagation as far back as 9400 BC at sites in the Jordan Valley. By the sixth and fifth millennia BC, fig cultivation was well established across the Levant and spreading into Anatolia and the eastern Mediterranean. Unlike most domesticated crops, figs are typically propagated vegetatively from cuttings rather than from seed, which means the adoption of fig cultivation required a specific understanding of plant reproduction.

Figs provided a reliable, storable source of nutrition and could be dried for long-term preservation — a practical asset for early farming communities. Their cultivation formed part of the broader emergence of orchard farming in the Near East, alongside olives, dates, and grapes, which together created the perennial crop foundation of Mediterranean agriculture. Fig remains are commonly found at Chalcolithic and early Bronze Age sites across the Levant, confirming their importance as a dietary staple and a trade commodity across this period.
